flashcards.front: What is the core principle of the SIMD architecture?
flashcards.back: A single instruction is executed simultaneously by multiple processing elements, each operating on its own distinct data item (Single Instruction, Multiple Data).

flashcards.front: What is the role of the Control Processor in a classical SIMD machine?
flashcards.back: It fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and broadcasts the same instruction to all Processing Elements (PEs) for synchronized, lockstep execution.

flashcards.front: What is lockstep execution in SIMD?
flashcards.back: All Processing Elements execute the same instruction at the same time, synchronized by the central control unit. This provides inherent synchronization and is ideal for regular data-parallel computations.

A modern implementation of SIMD within a processor core, using wide vector registers (e.g., 256-bit AVX) to hold multiple data elements so a single instruction can operate on all of them at once. Example: 8 parallel 32-bit float additions.

flashcards.front: How is the SIMD concept applied in GPU programming?
flashcards.back: GPUs execute threads in groups called warps (NVIDIA, 32 threads) or wavefronts (AMD). All threads in a warp execute the same instruction on different data in lockstep -- this is called SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads).
ILLIAC IV (1975, 64 processing elements) and Connection Machine CM-2 (1987, up to 65,536 processing elements). Both were processor arrays designed for data-parallel scientific computing.

flashcards.front: Name three modern CPU SIMD instruction set extensions.
flashcards.back: Intel MMX, SSE, and AVX (including AVX-512); ARM NEON and SVE. These provide packed SIMD operations within standard CPU cores for multimedia and scientific workloads.

flashcards.front: What is the main limitation of SIMD with conditional branches?
flashcards.back: In if-else statements, all PEs must execute both paths using masking (predication). PEs where the condition is false have their results discarded, wasting cycles. This is called branch divergence.

flashcards.front: What are the key advantages of SIMD over MIMD?
flashcards.back: Higher throughput for data-parallel tasks, better energy efficiency, lower control overhead (one control unit for many ALUs), and inherent synchronization (no explicit locks needed).

SIMD is poorly suited for irregular or data-dependent control flow (many if-else branches), task parallelism (different operations on different data), and workloads with non-uniform data access patterns.
